questions.prompt: Hardly ___ the meeting started when the fire alarm went off.
questions.explanation: Hardly + past perfect ... when + past simple.

questions.prompt: No sooner ___ than she started complaining.
Despite ___ the rain, the match continued.
If he ___ earlier, he wouldn't have missed the train.
Not only ___ he apologise, but he also denied everything.
It's about time you ___ to bed.
She acts as if she ___ the boss.
The report, ___ was published yesterday, caused a stir.
Were I you, I ___ reconsider.
He demanded that she ___ immediately.
The CEO's speech was widely criticised for its ___ tone.
The committee rejected the proposal, citing a lack of ___ evidence.
False friend: 'sensible' means:
The plan was abandoned due to ___ opposition.
Her explanation was so ___ that nobody understood it.
He gave a very ___ account of what happened.
The company is trying to ___ its image.
The new policy will ___ small businesses.
The results were ___ with earlier studies.
He is known for his ___ sense of humour.

sections.title: Reading comprehension
sections.passage: In an age of constant connectivity, the notion of 'digital minimalism' has gained traction among those who feel overwhelmed by endless notifications. Proponents argue that attention is a finite resource and that the default settings of many platforms are designed to maximise engagement rather than well-being. Yet critics contend that opting out can be a privilege: for some, social media is a vital tool for professional visibility, community building, or access to information that traditional institutions fail to provide. The debate, therefore, is less about technology itself than about agency—who controls it, who benefits from it, and how deliberately individuals choose to incorporate it into their lives.

questions.prompt: What is the main point of the last sentence?
options: The debate focuses on personal control and intentional use
correct: The debate focuses on personal control and intentional use

questions.prompt: The text suggests that leaving social media is equally easy for everyone.
questions.explanation: Opting out can be a privilege.

questions.prompt: What do critics argue?
options: Opting out can be a privilege
correct: Opting out can be a privilege

questions.prompt: What is the debate ultimately about?
Rewrite using the word 'little' (do not change it): I didn't expect the discussion to become so heated.
correct: Little did I expect the discussion to become so heated.
alternatives: Little did I expect the debate to become so heated.

questions.prompt: Finish the sentence: It's possible that he misunderstood what you said. He may ___.
Rewrite using 'not': It's unlikely that she will agree.
correct: She is not likely to agree.
alternatives: It is not likely that she will agree.

sections.title: Listening (placeholder)
sections.passage: (Listening text) Today I'm going to talk about why short-term measures are often more attractive to voters than long-term reforms. In the short run, politicians can offer quick benefits, such as temporary tax cuts or one-off subsidies, and people feel the impact immediately. Long-term reforms, however, usually involve trade-offs: they may require higher costs now in exchange for better outcomes later. That's why public support is essential, but it doesn't appear automatically. Leaders need to communicate clearly, not only about the expected benefits, but also about the costs and the timeline. Without transparency and trust, even well-designed reforms can fail because citizens assume that the burden will not be shared fairly. In other words, long-term change is as much a communication challenge as it is a policy challenge.

questions.prompt: According to the speaker, why are short-term measures popular?
options: They are easier to explain than anything else
questions.explanation: The speaker says people feel the impact immediately.

questions.prompt: What do long-term reforms usually involve?
options: Trade-offs and costs now for benefits later
correct: Trade-offs and costs now for benefits later
questions.explanation: They involve trade-offs and higher costs now for outcomes later.

questions.prompt: The speaker suggests that transparency and trust can influence whether reforms succeed.
questions.explanation: Without transparency and trust, reforms can fail.

questions.prompt: What is the final point of the talk?
options: Long-term change is both a policy and communication challenge
correct: Long-term change is both a policy and communication challenge
questions.explanation: The last sentence makes this point directly.

sections.title: Speaking (repeat / argument)
sections.instructions: Klikni na Přehrát a zkus větu zopakovat nahlas. Potom odpověz krátce vlastními slovy. Tahle část se nepočítá do skóre – je to trénink plynulosti, intonace a argumentace.

questions.prompt: Repeat: 'In the long run, effective reforms require both public trust and clear communication.'
questions.tts: In the long run, effective reforms require both public trust and clear communication.
questions.explanation: Zkus udržet přirozenou intonaci a důraz na key words.

questions.prompt: Repeat: 'Although the costs are immediate, the benefits may only become visible years later.'
questions.tts: Although the costs are immediate, the benefits may only become visible years later.

questions.prompt: Repeat: 'Without transparency, citizens are likely to assume the burden is unfair.'
questions.tts: Without transparency, citizens are likely to assume the burden is unfair.
questions.explanation: Pozor na výslovnost transparency / citizens / burden.

questions.prompt: Speak 2–3 sentences: Do you agree that communication is as important as policy? Give one reason.
questions.tts: Speak two or three sentences. Do you agree that communication is as important as policy? Give one reason.
questions.explanation: Otevřená odpověď – zkus použít because / therefore / however.

questions.prompt: Choose the most appropriate sentence for a formal email complaint:
options: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with the service provided.
correct: I am writing to express my dissatisfaction with the service provided.

questions.prompt: Write 1–2 sentences: Give an opinion on whether students should use AI to learn languages, and justify it.
